segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK
title: FEEDBACK LOOPS HIDE IN THE
DATA.
segments.body_html: The 2020 COVID testing failure: A public health system recommended concentrating resources in neighborhoods with high reported cases. But high reported cases were partly a function of prior testing rates. More testing revealed more cases, which attracted more resources. The system found a feedback loop and mistook it for a signal. Interventions based on this recommendation pulled resources away from under-tested areas where transmission was high but invisible.

beats.text: In spring 2020, as cities scaled COVID testing infrastructure, algorithmic systems were deployed to allocate resources. One system, trained on case counts and demographics, consistently recommended concentrating testing in neighborhoods with high reported cases. The logic seemed sound: high case rates predict high future rates; more testing catches more cases. The system was catastrophically wrong about causation. High reported cases were partly a function of prior testing rates themselves. More testing produced more confirmed cases, independent of true transmission. More cases then attracted more resources. The system found a feedback loop and mistook it for a signal. When followed, the recommendations pulled resources from under-tested neighborhoods where transmission was likely high but invisible, and poured them into already-saturated neighborhoods. This was not model incompetence. The model did exactly what it was trained to do. It is a story about what happens when an AI system recommends actions without being grounded in causal reasoning.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE STAKES
title: WHY AI SYSTEMS NEED TO REASON ABOUT
INTERVENTIONS.
segments.body_html:
What correlation teachesPrediction · Statistical patterns · Historical regularities
⇔
What correlation missesCausal mechanisms · Effects of intervention · Feedback loops

When an AI system recommends an action—allocate resources, adjust treatment, change policy—it makes an implicit causal claim: if you intervene this way, the world will change this way. Correlation cannot ground that claim. Only causal reasoning can.

beats.text: The gap between what machine learning learns and what it needs to know to make sound recommendations is the gap between seeing and doing. Most machine learning learns correlations: when X was high, Y tended to be high. But prediction and recommendation are different. When you recommend an action, you ask what happens if you intervene, if you force a variable to take a value it would not naturally take. This requires causal reasoning. The stakes are enormous. A system that confuses correlation with causation generates recommendations that seem justified by data but are actually harmful, strengthening the patterns it was trying to break. Understanding the difference between seeing and doing is foundational for building AI systems that reason responsibly about the consequences of action.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · CONCEPT 1
title: OBSERVING AND INTERVENING ARE
FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT.
segments.body_html:
ObserveLook at the data. In cases where X was x, what was Y? Learn the correlation. Use for prediction.
InterveneReach into the system and force X to be x regardless of what would naturally happen. Then ask what Y becomes. This is what recommendations really ask about.

beats.text: David Hume noticed something uncomfortable: no matter how many times you watch a rooster crow before sunrise—a thousand mornings, ten thousand—you cannot conclude the rooster causes the dawn. You have never observed what would happen if you forced the rooster to crow at midnight. Correlation, no matter how consistent, is not intervention. The difference is not subtle. When you observe, you look at the world as it naturally unfolds and condition on values that naturally occurred. When you intervene, you ask a counterfactual: what would happen if we forced this variable to this value, breaking whatever natural process would have generated it? Most statistical machinery that machine learning runs on can only answer the first question. But when an AI system makes a recommendation, it implicitly answers the second. This mismatch—learning one thing and using it to answer another—is where causal reasoning breaks down.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · CONCEPT 2
title: TWO WAYS TO CONDITION ON A
VARIABLE.
segments.body_html:
P(Y | X = x)Probability of Y given we observed X equals x. Conditional probability learned from historical data. Use for prediction, not for reasoning about interventions.
P(Y | do(X = x))Probability of Y given we intervened and forced X to equal x. The do( ) operator means forced. This answers: if we intervene this way, what will happen?

These expressions look nearly identical. The pipe means observed. The do( ) means forced. Confuse them, and every recommendation is built on unexamined assumptions about causation.

beats.text: Judea Pearl formalized Hume's intuition into mathematics, recognizing two fundamentally different ways to condition on a variable. Most statistics can only compute one. First: observation. Given our data, when X took value x, what was the distribution of Y? This is P(Y | X equals x), learned directly from historical cases. Second: intervention. If we forced X to take value x regardless of the natural process, what would Y become? Pearl's notation: P(Y | do(X equals x)), where do means forced. These are entirely different calculations. In observation, you condition on a value that actually occurred. In intervention, you reason counterfactually. Consider a hospital deciding whether to change treatment. A naive system might notice patients receiving treatment A had worse outcomes than those receiving B, and recommend switching. This confuses the two questions. Patients who received A may have been sicker to begin with—sicker patients receive stronger treatments. The observation tells you about selection bias, not the causal effect of treatment.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · CONCEPT 3
title: PEARL'S MOVE: MAKE THE DISTINCTION
COMPUTABLE.
segments.body_html: Formalizing causation: Pearl's notation—the do(X = x) operator—makes explicit that you are reasoning about intervention, not observation. This transforms Hume's philosophical insight into actionable mathematics using causal diagrams and formal inference.

beats.text: What Hume pointed to—that correlation never proves causation—is fundamental truth. Pearl transformed it into actionable mathematics two centuries later. Hume's rooster symptomizes a deeper problem: confusion between conditioning on an observed value and conditioning on a forced value. They look the same in English; they are mathematically different. Pearl's solution: create explicit notation for the distinction. The do-operator makes it formal and computable. When you write P(Y | do(X = x)), you explicitly state you are reasoning about intervention, not observation. This allows you to ask: if I force X to be x, what probability distribution does Y have? Crucially, it allows you to compute that using causal diagrams—directed acyclic graphs encoding causal structure. Once you have a causal diagram, you can apply algorithms like the backdoor criterion to determine exactly which variables you need to measure and adjust for to estimate the causal effect of an intervention. The problem Hume identified becomes solvable.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CONCEPT 4
title: CAUSAL GRAPHS REVEAL HIDDEN
PATHS.
segments.body_html:
ConfoundingA hidden common cause creating spurious association between treatment and outcome, biasing simple comparisons.
Backdoor pathsStatistical paths through the causal graph creating association without being the direct causal path of interest. They must be blocked to isolate true effect.
Adjustment setsVariables you condition on or match on to block all backdoor paths while leaving the front door open to the causal mechanism you want to measure.

beats.text: Once you commit to a causal graph—a directed acyclic graph encoding assumptions about which variables cause which—you can see what paths create spurious association between treatment and outcome. These are backdoor paths: they go backward from treatment to some common cause, then forward to outcome. They create association that looks causal but is not. Confounding happens when a variable influences both treatment and outcome. Simple comparison fails because you are not comparing like to like; sicker patients get stronger treatment, so treatment looks worse even if effective. The solution: block backdoor paths by conditioning on the right variables. This is the backdoor criterion—a set satisfies it if conditioning blocks all backdoor paths while leaving the direct path open. Once you identify an adjustment set satisfying the criterion, condition on those variables through matching, stratification, weighting, or regression. The association you measure becomes causal effect. Causal graphs transform a vague intuition into precise algorithm.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CONCEPT 5
title: GRAPH SURGERY IDENTIFIES WHICH VARIABLES TO
ADJUST.
segments.body_html:
Start with causal DAG→Delete edges into treatment→Find remaining paths to outcome→Which variables block backdoors?

Graph surgery answers: if I intervene on this variable, what other variables should I condition on? The answer is an adjustment set.

beats.text: Graph surgery is the operation that answers: what happens if I intervene? Start with your causal diagram. To find the causal effect of treatment on outcome, perform surgery: delete all edges going into the treatment variable. This removes all processes that would naturally determine treatment assignment. In the mutilated graph, any remaining path from treatment to outcome is one where treatment causally influences outcome. Any remaining path between treatment and confounders is a backdoor path. Now the question becomes: which variables, if you condition on them, will block all these backdoor paths? The answer is an adjustment set. The backdoor criterion gives a formal test: does conditioning on a set of variables block all backdoor paths while leaving the causal path open? If yes, conditioning on that set transforms observed association into true causal effect. Different adjustment sets may be valid—the criterion helps you choose among them. This algorithm bridges causal graphs and causal inference, telling you exactly what to measure and condition on.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · CONCEPT 6
title: REWARD HACKING: A CAUSAL FAILURE MODE, NOT A
TRAINING FAILURE.
segments.body_html:
Typical viewSystem optimizes a proxy; training overfit to the wrong objective.
Causal viewThe reward function is a causal lever. If the reward doesn't encode your actual goal, optimizing it has a predictable consequence: you get what you measured, not what you wanted.

Preventing reward hacking means redesigning the objective function to encode the causal pathway you actually want to reinforce.

beats.text: Reward hacking is typically framed as a training failure: the system overfit to a proxy objective. But from a causal perspective, it is not a training failure. It is a causal failure. You defined a reward function the system should optimize. The system optimizes it. If the reward function does not causally encode your actual objective, the system will pull causal levers that maximize reward without advancing your actual goal. The causal story is straightforward: you chose reward function R hoping it would be a good proxy for true objective G. But there are backdoor paths from R to outcomes that don't go through G, or R is determined by common causes affecting other variables you care about. When the system optimizes R, it amplifies these confounding effects. The solution is not better training. It is redesigning the objective function so optimizing it has the causal consequence you want. This sometimes means measuring harder things—things closer to your true goal. It means being explicit about causal pathways. If you want sustainable behavior, encode sustainability into the reward. If you want human welfare, include human feedback. The proxy must be chosen with causal reasoning about what optimizing it will actually cause.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · CONCEPT 7
title: MULTI-AGENT SYSTEMS SEPARATE CAUSAL FROM
EVALUATIVE.
segments.body_html:
Causal reasoning agentIdentifies causal pathways, estimates intervention effects, surfaces assumptions embedded in the causal model.
Value judgment agentApplies human preferences, objectives, and constraints to causal estimates. Decides which intervention to pursue.
Mandatory human gateFinal decision point where humans verify causal reasoning and retain veto power over recommendations.

beats.text: When an AI system recommends an intervention, it answers two very different questions: What is the causal effect of this intervention? And: Is this intervention worth doing? Conflating them is dangerous. One is empirical; the other is evaluative. A well-designed multi-agent causal system separates them. One agent is responsible for causal reasoning: building and testing the causal model, identifying adjustment sets, estimating effects, and making explicit assumptions baked into the causal graph. This agent answers: if we intervene this way, what will actually happen? A second agent is responsible for value judgment: taking those causal estimates and applying human preferences, constraints, and objectives. This agent answers: among interventions we could take, which should we? A third, mandatory component is a human decision gate. Before any significant intervention, a human verifies the causal reasoning and retains veto power. This structure ensures that causal expertise, value judgment, and human oversight are not competing for the same cognitive role. The system surfaces what it knows and doesn't know without pretending to know the values that should guide action.

segments.label: 10 / 12 · SYNTHESIS
title: THESE CONCEPTS FORM ONE COHERENT
FRAMEWORK.
segments.body_html:
Build a causal model→Identify causal effects via adjustment→Separate reasoning from judgment→Mandatory human gate

The COVID testing story shows what happens when you skip steps 1 and 2. The lesson generalizes: any AI system recommending actions must build a causal model, reason about effects, and embed that reasoning in a structure separating expertise from judgment while retaining human oversight.

beats.text: The concepts in this chapter—the distinction between observing and intervening, formal causal notation, causal graphs, backdoor paths, adjustment sets, reward hacking as causal failure, and multi-agent causal system structure—are not separate ideas. They are integrated pieces of a single framework for building AI systems that reason about interventions responsibly. The COVID testing failure exemplifies what happens when you skip causal reasoning. The system learned a correlation: more confirmed cases in neighborhoods attract more resources. It assumed optimizing resources to match cases would reduce transmission. It never asked causally: are more cases causing transmission, or is more testing causing cases? If it had built a causal model—even rough—it would identify the backdoor path from prior testing to resources through testing's confounding effect on case detection. It would adjust, or measure unobserved transmission instead. This framework is practical. Build causal models explicitly. Use graph surgery and backdoor criterion. Separate technical questions from evaluative ones. Insist on human gates. Each step prevents failure modes that cost public health resources and broke trust in algorithmic systems.

segments.label: 11 / 12 · THESIS
segments.body_html:
The difference between observing a correlation and intervening in a system is fundamental to causal reasoning, and it is precisely where most AI systems fail.

When you recommend an action, you make a causal claim: if we force this variable to this value, the world changes accordingly. Learning from data teaches correlations. It cannot teach causation without an explicit causal model. Building that model, reasoning about causal effects using formal tools like graphs and adjustment sets, and embedding that reasoning in a system separating expertise from judgment is what it takes to design an AI system that reasons about interventions responsibly.

beats.text: This chapter presents a thesis with two connected claims. First: the distinction between observing a value and intervening to force it is mathematically and conceptually fundamental. Second: most AI systems fail because they learn correlations without building explicit causal models, then use those correlations to make recommendations as if they were causal claims. The consequence is failure modes like COVID testing allocation: feedback loops are mistaken for signals, proxies are optimized without understanding their causal paths, and interventions with predictably harmful effects are deployed confidently. The solution is a framework. Build a causal model—a directed acyclic graph encoding assumptions about what causes what. Use causal diagrams to identify confounding variables. Apply the backdoor criterion to find adjustment sets. Condition on those variables and estimate causal effects. Separate the technical question from the evaluative question. Embed a mandatory human gate. This framework is tractable, teachable, and achievable without perfect knowledge. You begin with rough assumptions, test and refine them, remaining explicit about what you don't know. That honesty about causal ignorance and value trade-offs is what allows humans and AI to reason together about interventions in complex systems.

segments.label: 12 / 12 · CLOSE
segments.body_html:
CAUSAL REASONING//INTERVENTION VS OBSERVATION//AI RESPONSIBILITY

Causal Inference · Chapter 14 · Causal Agents

beats.text: You now have the foundations of causal reasoning needed to design AI systems that intervene responsibly in the world. The next chapter deepens this work, showing how these ideas scale to more complex scenarios.
